name: Maternal serum hCG at peak
tier_rationale: Tier A from Cole 2010 review and standard reproductive endocrinology: hCG peaks at ~50,000-200,000 mIU/mL around 10 weeks; the peak rescues the corpus luteum.

source_check.source_citation: Endotext, Endocrinology of Pregnancy (NCBI Bookshelf NBK278962)
source_check.quote: The average peak hCG level is approximately 110,000 mIU/mL and occurs at 10 weeks' gestation
source_check.extracted_value: Average peak hCG ~110,000 mIU/mL at 10 weeks; StatPearls notes many normal pregnancies reach >100,000 mIU/mL at the 8-11 week peak. Stored central 100,000 (range 50,000-200,000) is consistent.

name: Gestational week of peak hCG
tier_rationale: Tier A from Cole 2010: hCG peak ~10 weeks, before placental progesterone production assumes corpus luteum's role.

source_check.quote: As shown in Tables 2 and 3 hCG reaches a peak at 10 weeks of gestation, or almost one month after progesterone promotion is complete, then continues to be produced through the length of pregnancy.
source_check.extracted_value: Fulltext explicitly states hCG reaches a peak at 10 weeks of gestation, matching the stored central value of 10 weeks (range 8-12).

name: Maternal serum hCG at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Cole 2010: hCG declines from peak to ~10,000 mIU/mL at term.

source_check.extracted_value: Cleveland Clinic reference table gives a third-trimester (25-40 weeks) hCG range of 3,640-117,000 mIU/mL, which brackets the stored term value of 10,000 (5,000-20,000) mIU/mL.

name: Maternal serum placental lactogen at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Handwerger 1991 review: hPL rises linearly through gestation, ~5-10 ug/mL at term. Drives maternal insulin resistance and lipolysis.

source_check.quote: In the third trimester, the level reaches 4.50-12.80 mcg/mL.
source_check.extracted_value: Cleveland Clinic gives a third-trimester hPL reference range of 4.50-12.80 mcg/mL; Endotext review gives a term rise to ~3.5-25 ug/mL. Stored central 7 (5-10) ug/mL sits within both.

name: Maternal serum progesterone at term
tier_rationale: Tier A from Tulchinsky 1972 longitudinal study: progesterone rises through gestation, ~150 ng/mL at term, placental-derived after T1.

source_check.source_citation: Endotext, Endocrinology of Pregnancy (NCBI Bookshelf NBK278962)
source_check.quote: At term, progesterone concentrations can range from 100-300 ng/mL
source_check.extracted_value: Endotext states term progesterone ranges 100-300 ng/mL; stored central 150 (100-200) ng/mL is consistent and sits within this band.

name: Maternal serum estradiol at term
tier_rationale: Tier A from Tulchinsky 1972: estradiol rises through gestation to ~10-25 ng/mL at term; placental-fetal unit synthesis.

source_check.source_citation: Endotext, Endocrinology of Pregnancy (NCBI Bookshelf NBK278962)
source_check.quote: Following fertilization, 17β-estradiol increases gradually to a range of 6-30 ng/mL at term
source_check.extracted_value: Endotext states 17beta-estradiol rises to 6-30 ng/mL at term; stored central 14 (8-25) ng/mL sits within this range.

name: Maternal serum estriol at term
tier_rationale: Tier A from Tulchinsky 1972: estriol is uniquely a fetal-placental co-product (placenta lacks 16-alpha-hydroxylase); ~10 ng/mL at term.

source_check.source_citation: Endotext, Endocrinology of Pregnancy (NCBI Bookshelf NBK278962)
source_check.quote: estriol increases gradually to a range of approximately 10-30 ng/ml at term
source_check.extracted_value: Endotext states estriol rises to approximately 10-30 ng/ml at term; stored central 10 (6-20) ng/mL brackets the lower end of this range.

name: Maternal serum placental lactogen, non-pregnant baseline
tier_rationale: Tier B: hPL is exclusively placental, so non-pregnant baseline is operationally zero (below standard assay sensitivity ~0.1 ug/mL). Handwerger 1991 documents the rise from undetectable to term value.

source_check.quote: If you're not pregnant, the level is 0.00-0.10 mcg/mL (micrograms per milliliter).
source_check.extracted_value: Cleveland Clinic states the non-pregnant hPL level is 0.00-0.10 mcg/mL, matching the stored operational baseline of 0 (0-0.1) ug/mL.

name: Maternal serum progesterone, non-pregnant baseline (mid-luteal)
tier_rationale: Tier B from Tulchinsky 1972: mid-luteal progesterone ~5-20 ng/mL pre-pregnancy; corpus luteum supplies progesterone for the first ~8 weeks before placental takeover.

source_check.source_citation: Endotext, Endocrinology of Pregnancy (NCBI Bookshelf NBK278962)
source_check.quote: progesterone concentrations rise from about 1-2 ng/mL on the day of the LH surge to a plateau of approximately 10-35 ng/mL over the subsequent 7 days.
source_check.extracted_value: Endotext states luteal-phase progesterone reaches a plateau of approximately 10-35 ng/mL; stored mid-luteal baseline 10 (5-20) ng/mL sits at the lower end of this range.

name: Maternal serum estradiol, non-pregnant baseline (mid-luteal)
tier_rationale: Tier B from Tulchinsky 1972 / O'Leary 1991: mid-luteal estradiol ~50-250 pg/mL (0.05-0.25 ng/mL); placental output rises through gestation to ~100x baseline at term.

source_check.source_citation: Endotext, Endocrinology of Pregnancy (NCBI Bookshelf NBK278962)
source_check.quote: Concentrations of 17β-estradiol are less than 0.1 ng/mL during the follicular phase of the cycle and reach about 0.4 ng/mL during the luteal phase of normal menstrual cycles
source_check.extracted_value: Endotext states non-pregnant 17beta-estradiol is <0.1 ng/mL (follicular) and ~0.4 ng/mL (luteal). Stored mid-luteal baseline 0.1 (0.05-0.25) ng/mL is consistent in magnitude; the source's luteal point (0.4) is above the stored high.

name: Serum relaxin in first trimester
tier_rationale: Tier B from Conrad 2001 (Endocrinology review of relaxin physiology): corpus luteum-derived serum relaxin peaks in the late first trimester at ~1 ng/mL, then declines through T2/T3 as luteal function wanes. Relaxin is the proximate cause of the pregnancy-induced renal vasodilation captured by the GFR/RPF trajectories.

source_check.source_citation: Conrad & Baker, Maternal vasodilation in pregnancy: the emerging role of relaxin (Am J Physiol, PMC3154715)
source_check.quote: Relaxin is detectable in the circulation of women during the luteal phase, and, if conception occurs, serum concentrations rapidly rise, reaching a peak of ∼1 ng/ml at the end of the first trimester.
source_check.extracted_value: Peer-reviewed OA review states serum relaxin peaks at ~1 ng/ml at the end of the first trimester; stored central 1.0 (0.6-1.5) ng/mL matches.

applicability.population: Healthy singleton pregnancies, first trimester (peak around weeks 10–12)

notes: T1 peak corresponds to corpus-luteum dominance; placental contribution is small.

name: Serum leptin at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Hardie et al. 1997 Clin Endocrinol longitudinal cohort: serum leptin rises ~2–3× pre-pregnancy values across gestation, peaking in T3 at ~30 ng/mL. Both adipocyte and placental sources contribute (placenta expresses leptin). Replicated across modern pregnancy leptin studies; the central value scales with maternal BMI.
notes: Leptin falls rapidly after delivery — consistent with the substantial placental contribution to circulating levels in pregnancy.

name: Placental growth hormone at term
tier_rationale: Tier C from Eriksson et al. 1989 Br J Obstet Gynaecol: placental growth hormone (GH-V, the variant produced by the syncytiotrophoblast) accumulates non-pulsatily across pregnancy, replacing maternal pituitary GH; term serum levels centre ~10–20 ng/mL. The non-pulsatile secretion pattern distinguishes the placental variant from pituitary GH on serial sampling. Assay specificity (GH-V vs GH-N cross-reactivity) is the main source of inter-study variability.
notes: Placental GH is the dominant maternal-circulation GH from mid-pregnancy onward; drives the late-pregnancy IGF-1 rise.
